Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

A neural network that reaches 99% accuracy recognizing handwritten characters can fall apart completely the moment you ask it to read a paragraph of text.
That’s not a training bug. It’s a design problem. Every kind of data carries its own hidden structure, and a neural network only performs well when its architecture is built to notice that structure. Today’s article sets up an idea the rest of this series will build on: images, text, and sequences each need a specialized design, not a bigger version of the same one.
That’s not a training bug. It’s a design problem. Every kind of data carries its own hidden structure, and a neural network only performs well when its architecture is built to notice that structure. Today’s article sets up an idea the rest of this series will build on: images, text, and sequences each need a specialized design, not a bigger version of the same one.
Fully-connected Networks Make One Big Assumption
Fully-connected, or feedforward networks only work in certain scenarios. In these networks, every input connects to every neuron in the next layer, each connection carrying its own weight, a learnable number that scales how much that input matters. That design treats every input value as equally important and completely independent of the ones next to it. Feed it a list of features like a house’s square footage, number of bedrooms, and age, and that assumption holds up fine. There’s no meaningful “neighbor” relationship between square footage and bedroom count, so connecting everything to everything doesn’t waste anything.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Images Break That Assumption
A photograph is not a list of unrelated numbers. It’s a grid of pixels, and the pixels sitting right next to each other are exactly what forms an edge, a curve, or a texture. Flattening an image into one long list of pixel values throws away that neighbor information entirely, and connecting every single pixel to every neuron in the next layer gets expensive fast. The code below shows just how fast, using nothing more than a tiny, low-resolution image.
image_pixels = 28 * 28 * 3 # a small 28x28 color image, flattened into a list
hidden_neurons = 128weights_needed = image_pixels * hidden_neurons
biases_needed = hidden_neurons
total_params = weights_needed + biases_needed
print(f"Flattened pixel values: {image_pixels}")
print(f"Weights needed for just this one layer: {weights_needed}")
print(f"Total parameters (weights and biases) in just this one layer: {total_params}")
Running this prints:
Flattened pixel values: 2352
Weights needed for just this one layer: 301056
Total parameters (weights and biases) in just this one layer: 301184That’s over 300,000 parameters, the individual numbers a neural network adjusts during training, for one modest layer processing a tiny image, and none of that math even uses the fact that nearby pixels are related. Real photos run into the millions of pixels. A specialized design that looks at small local patches of an image at a time, instead of connecting every pixel to every neuron, needs far fewer parameters and actually pays attention to the structure that matters.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Text and Sequences Break It Differently
Language has a different kind of structure. The phrases “dog bites man” and “man bites dog” use identical words but mean opposite things, because order carries the meaning. A fully-connected network has no built-in concept of order. It just sees a fixed set of inputs, arranged however they happened to be listed. Sequences also vary in length: one sentence has five words, another has fifty, and a network built to expect one fixed length can’t automatically handle the other.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Sequential data like text, audio, and prices over time needs a design that reads information step by step, carrying forward what it has seen so far, or that can weigh how much attention to pay to each earlier piece when producing the next part of the output. That’s a completely different problem than recognizing a pattern in a fixed grid of pixels, so it calls for a completely different architecture too.
Why Specialization Beats One Big Network
It’s tempting to think a bigger, more powerful fully-connected network could eventually learn to handle images or text just by throwing enough neurons and training data at the problem. In practice, that approach wastes enormous amounts of data and computing time relearning structure that could have been built into the architecture from the start. A network built for images already assumes, before training even begins, that nearby pixels tend to be related. A network built for sequences already assumes that order matters. That built-in assumption, often called an inductive bias, meaning a rule baked into the design that shapes what patterns the network finds easiest to learn, means the network needs far less data and far less training time to become truly good at its job.
This is why neural networks split into distinct architectures rather than settling on one universal design. A network that excels at classifying photos usually performs poorly on raw text, and a network built for language usually isn’t the right tool for reading an X-ray, even though both are technically neural networks built from the same neurons, weights, and activation functions, the functions that decide what a neuron outputs, covered earlier in this series. The differences show up in how the layers connect and what assumptions get wired into that structure.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Conclusion
A neural network only learns well when its architecture matches the structure hidden in its data. Fully-connected networks assume every input is independent, which works fine for simple tabular data but wastes enormous amounts of parameters and training data on images, where neighboring pixels matter, and fails to capture the order that gives sequences like text their meaning. That mismatch is exactly why specialized designs exist: architectures built specifically to notice spatial patterns in images, and others built specifically to track order and context across a sequence. The next few articles dig into exactly how those specialized designs work, starting with the one built for images.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Tomorrow, in Day 24, we’ll look at convolutional neural networks and how sliding small filters across an image lets a neural network detect edges, textures, and patterns far more efficiently than connecting every pixel to every neuron.
